glossary.def: Any divergence between the data distribution a model was trained on and the one it serves in production. It decomposes into covariate shift (P(x) moves), label/prior shift (P(y) moves), and concept drift (P(y|x) moves), each demanding a different fix.

A change in the input distribution P(x) while the input-to-label rule P(y|x) stays fixed. It is visible from features alone and can sometimes be corrected by importance weighting without any new labels.

A change in the relationship P(y|x) itself, so the learned function becomes wrong rather than merely under-sampled. It can be invisible in the inputs and surface only as falling accuracy, which is why it needs error monitoring and fresh labelled data to fix.

A scalar drift measure: bin a score on a reference period (expected proportions E_i) and on a live period (actual A_i), then sum (A_i − E_i)·ln(A_i/E_i) over bins. It equals the symmetrized KL divergence; the rule of thumb is <0.1 stable, 0.1–0.25 moderate, >0.25 significant.
The PSI formula applied to a single input feature rather than to the model's output score. A drifting CSI with a stable PSI says an input moved but the score has so far absorbed it.

An online concept-drift detector that treats per-example errors as a Bernoulli stream, tracks the running error rate and its standard deviation, and alarms when the rate climbs two (warning) or three (drift) standard deviations above its best-so-far minimum.

The baseline distribution a monitor compares the live stream against. A fixed reference (the training set) answers 'is the model still valid?'; a sliding reference detects change but normalizes away slow incremental drift, letting a model boil like a frog.

How often a deployed model is refit. Too often wastes compute; too rarely carries accumulating decay. Balancing amortized retrain cost against carried decay gives a square-root optimum T* = sqrt(2·c_retrain/d), the economic-order-quantity shape.

Gama, J., Medas, P., Castillo, G. & Rodrigues, P. (2004). Learning with Drift Detection (DDM). SBIA 2004, LNCS 3171.
Bifet, A. & Gavalda, R. (2007). Learning from Time-Changing Data with Adaptive Windowing (ADWIN). SIAM SDM 2007.
Gretton, A., Borgwardt, K., Rasch, M., Scholkopf, B. & Smola, A. (2012). A Kernel Two-Sample Test (MMD). JMLR 13.
Quinonero-Candela, J., Sugiyama, M., Schwaighofer, A. & Lawrence, N. (eds.) (2009). Dataset Shift in Machine Learning. MIT Press.
